docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Luz Language

A lightweight, interpreted programming language designed to be simple, readable, and easy to learn.

Download Get Started →

No Python required

docs.title: A taste of Luz
docs.text:
name = listen("What is your name? ")
write($"Hello {name}!")

for i = 1 to 5 {
    if even(i) {
        write($"{i} is even")
    } else {
        write($"{i} is odd")
    }
}

docs.title: Why Luz?
docs.text:
⚡ No boilerplate

Variables need no declaration keyword. Blocks use { }.

📖 Readable syntax

Keywords read like English: for i = 1 to 10, attempt / rescue.

🏗️ Full OOP

Classes, inheritance, method overriding, and super.

🔧 First-class functions

Lambdas, closures, and higher-order functions.

🛡️ Helpful errors

Every error includes the source line number.

📦 Standard library

Math, random, I/O, system, and clock — all built in.

docs.title: Quick example
docs.text:
class Animal {
    function init(self, name) {
        self.name = name
    }
    function speak(self) {
        write($"{self.name} says hello!")
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    function speak(self) {
        super.speak()
        write("(woof!)")
    }
}

d = Dog("Rex")
d.speak()

docs.title: Features at a glance
docs.text: Feature Syntax Variable x = 10 Null coalescing x = value ?? "default" Format string $"Hello {name}" List comprehension [x * x for x in list] Membership 3 in [1, 2, 3] String repeat "ha" * 3 For range for i = 1 to 10 { } For each for item in list { } Lambda fn(x) => x * 2 Class class Dog extends Animal { } Error handling attempt { } rescue (e) { } Import import "math"

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Luz follows a classic three-stage interpreter pipeline:

Source code (text)
      |
   [Lexer]         luz/lexer.py
      |
 Token stream
      |
   [Parser]        luz/parser.py
      |
  AST (tree)
      |
 [Interpreter]     luz/interpreter.py
      |
   Result

docs.title: Lexer (luz/lexer.py)
docs.text:
Converts raw source text into a flat list of Token objects.

  • Handles integers, floats (including .5 notation), strings, format strings, identifiers, keywords, and all operators.
  • Tracks line numbers on every token so errors can report the correct source line.
  • Resolves string escape sequences (\n, \t, \\, \") at this stage.
  • Format strings store the raw template (e.g. "Hello {name}") — expression parsing happens later in the parser.

docs.title: Parser (luz/parser.py)
docs.text:
Consumes the token stream and builds an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) using a recursive descent parser.

Operator precedence is enforced through a chain of parsing functions, each calling the next higher-precedence level:

logical_or → logical_and → logical_not → comparison
           → arithmetic → term → power → factor

Each node type (BinOpNode, IfNode, CallNode, ClassDefNode, etc.) is a plain Python class defined at the top of the file. Nodes hold references to their child nodes, forming a tree.

Format string parsing: the lexer stores raw template text; the parser splits it on {...} (tracking brace depth for nesting), then sub-parses each embedded expression using a fresh Lexer + Parser.

docs.title: Interpreter (luz/interpreter.py)
docs.text:
Walks the AST using the Visitor pattern: visit(node) dispatches to visit_IfNode, visit_BinOpNode, etc. based on the node's class name.

Scope is managed through a chain of Environment objects. Each block or function call creates a new environment linked to its parent, enabling proper variable scoping and closures.

OOP is implemented through:

Object Role LuzClass Stores the method dictionary and a reference to the parent class LuzInstance Stores the instance's attribute dictionary and a reference to its class LuzSuperProxy Wraps an instance + a parent class; resolves super.method() calls LuzFunction A named function with a closure LuzLambda An anonymous function (short fn(x) => expr or long fn(x) { body })

Method calls automatically inject self and super into the method's local scope.

Control flow signals (return, break, continue) are implemented as Python exceptions that propagate up the call stack and are caught at the appropriate node handler.

docs.title: Error system (luz/exceptions.py)
docs.text:
All errors inherit from LuzError:

LuzError
├── SyntaxFault       (lexer / parser errors)
├── SemanticFault     (type errors, undefined variables, wrong argument count…)
│   ├── AttributeNotFoundFault
│   └── ArityFault
├── RuntimeFault      (division by zero, index out of bounds…)
├── CastFault         (failed type conversion)
└── UserFault         (raised by the alert keyword)

Every error carries a line attribute that is attached when the error propagates through visit().

docs.title: File overview
docs.text:
luz-lang/
├── main.py               # Entry point: REPL and file execution
├── luz/
│   ├── tokens.py         # TokenType enum and Token class
│   ├── lexer.py          # Lexer: text → tokens
│   ├── parser.py         # Parser: tokens → AST + all AST node classes
│   ├── interpreter.py    # Interpreter: executes the AST
│   └── exceptions.py     # Full error class hierarchy
├── tests/
│   └── test_suite.py     # Test suite
├── docs/                 # This documentation (MkDocs)
├── vscode-luz/           # VS Code syntax highlighting extension
└── examples/             # Example programs

docs.title: I/O
docs.text:
Function Description write(...) Print one or more values to stdout, separated by spaces listen(prompt) Print prompt and read a line from stdin. Numbers are auto-converted 
write("hello")              # hello
write(1, 2, 3)              # 1 2 3
name = listen("Name: ")

docs.title: Type inspection
docs.text: Function Description Example typeof(value) Returns the type name as a string typeof(42) → "int" instanceof(obj, Class) True if obj is an instance of Class or a subclass instanceof(d, Animal) → true

docs.title: Type casting
docs.text:
Function Description to_int(v) Convert to integer (truncates floats, parses strings) to_float(v) Convert to float to_str(v) Convert to string to_bool(v) Convert to boolean to_num(v) Convert string to int or float (auto-detects)

Raises CastFault if the conversion is not possible.

to_int(3.9)      # 3
to_int("42")     # 42
to_float("2.5")  # 2.5
to_str(true)     # "true"
to_bool(0)       # false
to_bool("")      # false
to_num("10")     # 10
to_num("3.14")   # 3.14

docs.title: Math
docs.text: Function Description Example abs(x) Absolute value abs(-7) → 7 sqrt(x) Square root sqrt(16) → 4.0 floor(x) Round down to integer floor(3.9) → 3 ceil(x) Round up to integer ceil(3.1) → 4 round(x, digits?) Round to N decimal places (default 0) round(3.567, 2) → 3.57 clamp(x, low, high) Force x into the range [low, high] clamp(15, 0, 10) → 10 max(a, b, ...) Maximum of values or a list max(3, 8, 2) → 8 min(a, b, ...) Minimum of values or a list min([5, 1, 9]) → 1 sign(x) Returns -1, 0, or 1 sign(-5) → -1 odd(x) True if x is odd odd(3) → true even(x) True if x is even even(4) → true

docs.title: Lists
docs.text:
Function Description len(list) Number of elements append(list, value) Add element to the end (modifies in place) pop(list) Remove and return the last element pop(list, index) Remove and return the element at index 
nums = [10, 20, 30]
append(nums, 40)
write(len(nums))      # 4
write(pop(nums))      # 40
write(pop(nums, 0))   # 10

docs.title: Dictionaries
docs.text:
Function Description len(dict) Number of key-value pairs keys(dict) Returns a list of all keys values(dict) Returns a list of all values remove(dict, key) Remove key and return its value 
d = {"a": 1, "b": 2}
write(keys(d))         # ["a", "b"]
write(values(d))       # [1, 2]
write(remove(d, "a"))  # 1

docs.title: Strings
docs.text: Function Description Example len(s) String length len("hi") → 2 trim(s) Remove surrounding whitespace trim(" hi ") → "hi" uppercase(s) Convert to uppercase uppercase("hi") → "HI" lowercase(s) Convert to lowercase lowercase("HI") → "hi" swap(s, old, new) Replace all occurrences of old with new swap("aXb", "X", "-") → "a-b" split(s, sep?) Split into list (default sep: whitespace) split("a,b", ",") → ["a","b"] join(sep, list) Join list into a string join("-", ["a","b"]) → "a-b" contains(s, sub) True if sub is in s contains("hello", "ell") → true begins(s, prefix) True if s starts with prefix begins("hello", "he") → true ends(s, suffix) True if s ends with suffix ends("hello", "lo") → true find(s, sub) Index of first occurrence, -1 if not found find("hello", "ll") → 2 count(s, sub) Count occurrences of sub in s count("banana", "a") → 3

docs.title: Download
docs.text:
🪟 Download for Windows

Installer · No dependencies

🐧 Download for Linux

tar.gz · luz + ray binaries

docs.title: Windows — What's included
docs.text:
• Full Luz interpreter (luz.exe)
  • ray package manager (ray.exe)
  • luz and ray added to your system PATH automatically
  • Standard libraries pre-installed (luz-math, luz-random, luz-io, luz-system)
  • No Python required

docs.title: Linux — Setup after download
docs.text:
tar -xzf luz-*-linux.tar.gz
sudo mv luz ray /usr/local/bin/
luz program.luz

docs.title: After installing
docs.text:
luz program.luz        # run a file
luz                    # open the interactive REPL
ray install user/pkg   # install a package

docs.title: Release history
docs.text: Version Highlights v1.14.0 File I/O built-ins, luz-io library, luz-system library, Linux binary v1.13.0 File I/O built-ins, luz-io library, ternary fix v1.12.0 switch/match, variadic functions, default parameters, multiple return values, ternary operator v1.10.0 luz-random library, compound assignment (+= etc.), negative indexing v1.8.0 Lambdas, OOP, format strings, modules, luz-math library, standalone installer

docs.title: Ray Package Manager
docs.text: Ray is the official package manager for Luz. It installs packages from GitHub directly into your project.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
ray init                   # create a luz.json in the current directory
ray install user/repo      # install a package from GitHub
ray list                   # list installed packages
ray remove package-name    # remove a package

docs.title: Installing a package
docs.text:
ray install Elabsurdo984/luz-utils

Ray downloads the repository as a zip, extracts it, and places it in luz_modules/<name>/. After installing, import it normally:

import "utils"

docs.title: Creating a library
docs.text:
Any Luz project can be published as a package. The only requirement is a luz.json file at the root.

luz.json 

{
  "name": "my-library",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "My Luz library",
  "author": "yourname",
  "main": "my-library.luz"
}

  • name — the name users will use to import your library.
  • main — the entry point file that gets executed on import "my-library".

Recommended structure

my-library/
├── luz.json
├── my-library.luz       ← entry point (imports the modules below)
├── core.luz
├── utils.luz
└── helpers.luz

my-library.luz 

import "core.luz"
import "utils.luz"
import "helpers.luz"

Users install it with:

ray install yourname/my-library

And use it with:

import "my-library"

docs.title: Project manifest
docs.text:
ray init creates a luz.json for your project:

{
  "name": "my-project",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "description": "",
  "author": ""
}

docs.title: luz_modules/
docs.text:
Installed packages live in luz_modules/ inside your project. Add this folder to .gitignore if you don't want to commit dependencies.

luz_modules/
├── my-library/
│   ├── luz.json
│   └── my-library.luz

docs.title: Versioning
docs.text: Luz follows Semantic Versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH).

docs.title: When each number bumps
docs.text:
Change Type Example Bug fix, no new syntax PATCH 1.15.0 → 1.15.1 New feature, built-in, or stdlib module MINOR 1.15.0 → 1.16.0 Syntax change that breaks existing code MAJOR 1.x.x → 2.0.0

Key rule: if someone has .luz code written today and it fails after an update, that update must be a MAJOR. If the update fixes a bug — PATCH. If it only adds things — MINOR.

docs.title: Release flow
docs.text:
Releases are organized around GitHub Milestones. Each milestone groups related issues under a target version (e.g., v1.16.0 – Methods Update).

develop  ──●──●──●──────●──●──●──────●─→
             feat      feat          feat
master   ──────────────●────────────────●─→
                     v1.16.0         v1.17.0
  1. A milestone is created with a name and target version (e.g., v1.16.0 – Methods Update).
  2. Related issues are assigned to that milestone.
  3. Work happens on develop (or feature/* branches that merge into develop).
  4. When all milestone issues are closed → merge develop into master → tag vX.Y.Z → GitHub Release.
  5. The milestone is then closed.

Hotfixes go directly to master, get a PATCH bump, and are tagged immediately — no milestone needed.

docs.title: When to open a new milestone
docs.text:
A good milestone has 2–4 related issues that together represent a coherent improvement. Avoid:

  • Single-issue milestones (use a PATCH or just a PR instead).
  • Milestones with 10+ issues (they never close and block releases).

docs.title: Branch strategy
docs.text: Branch Purpose master Stable, released code. Every commit here is a tag. develop Integration branch. Features merge here first. feature/* Short-lived branches for individual issues.

docs.title: Current version
docs.text: The current stable release is always the latest tag on master. Check the releases page for the full changelog.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Luz requires Python 3.8 or higher and has no external dependencies.

docs.title: Clone the repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Elabsurdo984/luz-lang.git
cd luz-lang

That's it — no build step, no package manager, no virtual environment needed.

docs.title: VS Code syntax highlighting
docs.text:
A syntax highlighting extension for VS Code is included in the vscode-luz/ folder.

  1. Copy the folder to ~/.vscode/extensions/
  2. Restart VS Code

Luz files (.luz) will now have keyword highlighting.

docs.title: Interactive REPL
docs.text:
Run main.py with no arguments to open the interactive shell:

python main.py

Luz Interpreter v1.8.0 - Type 'exit' to terminate
Luz > x = 10
Luz > write(x * 2)
20
Luz > name = "world"
Luz > write($"Hello {name}!")
Hello world!
Luz > exit

The REPL evaluates one statement at a time. Multi-line constructs (functions, classes, loops) are not supported in the REPL — use a file for those.

docs.title: Run a file
docs.text:
python main.py program.luz

Any .luz file can be executed this way. The interpreter runs the file from top to bottom and exits when it reaches the end.

docs.title: Run the test suite
docs.text:
python tests/test_suite.py

All built-in tests will run and report pass/fail.

docs.title: Lists
docs.text:
A list is an ordered, mutable sequence of values.

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
mixed  = [1, "two", 3.0, true, null]
empty  = []

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text:
Zero-based. Negative indices count from the end:

write(fruits[0])    # apple
write(fruits[-1])   # cherry  (last element)
write(fruits[-2])   # banana

docs.title: Assignment by index
docs.text: fruits[1] = "mango"

docs.title: Iteration
docs.text:
for fruit in fruits {
    write(fruit)
}

docs.title: List built-ins
docs.text: Function Description len(list) Number of elements append(list, value) Add element to the end pop(list) Remove and return the last element pop(list, index) Remove and return element at index

docs.title: List dot methods
docs.text:
All list operations are also available as dot methods:

nums = [1, 2, 3]

nums.append(4)          # [1, 2, 3, 4]
last = nums.pop()       # 4  — removes and returns the last element
write(nums.len())       # 3
write(nums.contains(2)) # true
write(nums.contains(9)) # false

words = ["hello", "world"]
write(words.join(", "))  # hello, world
Method Equivalent built-in list.append(value) append(list, value) list.pop() pop(list) list.pop(index) pop(list, index) list.len() len(list) list.contains(value) — list.join(sep) join(sep, list)

docs.title: Strings
docs.text:
Strings support indexing and negative indices:

s = "hello"
write(s[0])    # h
write(s[-1])   # o

docs.title: Dictionaries
docs.text:
A dictionary maps keys to values. Keys must be strings or numbers.

person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}

docs.title: Access and assignment
docs.text:
write(person["name"])   # Alice
person["age"] = 31
person["city"] = "Madrid"

docs.title: Iteration
docs.text:
Iterating over a dictionary yields its keys:

for key in person {
    write($"{key}: {person[key]}")
}

docs.title: Dictionary built-ins
docs.text: Function Description len(dict) Number of key-value pairs keys(dict) Returns a list of all keys values(dict) Returns a list of all values remove(dict, key) Remove key and return its value

docs.title: if / elif / else
docs.text:
x = 15

if x > 20 {
    write("big")
} elif x > 10 {
    write("medium")
} else {
    write("small")
}
  • elif and else are optional.
  • Braces { } are always required.

docs.title: Ternary operator
docs.text:
A compact inline conditional that returns a value:

label = "big" if x > 10 else "small"
write(label)

Ternaries can be chained:

grade = "A" if score >= 90 else "B" if score >= 80 else "C"

docs.title: while
docs.text:
i = 0
while i < 5 {
    write(i)
    i += 1
}

docs.title: for — range loop
docs.text:
Iterates from start to end inclusive:

for i = 1 to 10 {
    write(i)
}

docs.title: for — for-each loop
docs.text:
Iterates over lists, string characters, or dictionary keys:

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
for fruit in fruits {
    write(fruit)
}

for ch in "hello" {
    write(ch)
}

for key in {"a": 1, "b": 2} {
    write(key)
}

docs.title: switch
docs.text:
Compares a value against multiple cases. Each case can match multiple values.

switch x {
    case 1 { write("one") }
    case 2, 3 { write("two or three") }
    else { write("other") }
}
  • Cases are checked in order; only the first match runs.
  • else is optional and acts as the fallback.
  • The subject is compared with ==.

docs.title: match
docs.text:
Like switch but is an expression — it returns a value.

label = match x {
    0 => "zero"
    1 => "one"
    2, 3 => "two or three"
    _ => "many"
}
  • _ is the wildcard — matches anything and must be last.
  • Each arm is pattern => expression.
  • Multiple patterns per arm: 2, 3 => "two or three".

docs.title: break / continue / pass
docs.text:
for i = 1 to 10 {
    if i == 5 { break }       # exit the loop
    if i == 3 { continue }    # skip to next iteration
    write(i)
}

if true {
    pass    # no-op placeholder
}

docs.title: attempt / rescue
docs.text:
Wrap code that might fail in an attempt block. If an error is raised, execution jumps to the rescue block, where you can inspect the error message:

attempt {
    x = 10 / 0
} rescue (error) {
    write("Caught: " + error)
}

The variable in rescue (error) holds the error message as a string.

docs.title: alert
docs.text:
Raise a custom error with the alert keyword:

function divide(a, b) {
    if b == 0 {
        alert "Cannot divide by zero"
    }
    return a / b
}

attempt {
    result = divide(5, 0)
} rescue (e) {
    write(e)   # Cannot divide by zero
}

alert accepts any expression:

alert "Something went wrong"
alert $"Value {x} is out of range"

docs.title: Error types
docs.text:
All Luz errors fall into four categories:

Type Raised when SyntaxFault The source code is malformed (lexer or parser) SemanticFault A name is undefined, wrong number of arguments, wrong type, etc. RuntimeFault Division by zero, index out of bounds, invalid operation UserFault Raised explicitly with alert CastFault A type conversion with to_int, to_float, etc. fails

Every error message includes the source line number.

docs.title: Nested attempt blocks
docs.text:
attempt blocks can be nested. The innermost matching rescue handles the error:

attempt {
    attempt {
        alert "inner error"
    } rescue (e) {
        write("Inner caught: " + e)
        alert "re-raised"
    }
} rescue (e) {
    write("Outer caught: " + e)
}

docs.title: Errors not caught
docs.text: If an error propagates out of all attempt blocks, the interpreter prints the error and stops execution.

docs.title: Format Strings
docs.text:
A format string is a string prefixed with $ that can embed expressions inside { }.

name = "Alice"
age = 30
write($"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old!")

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
$"text {expression} more text"

Any valid Luz expression can go inside { }:

write($"2 + 2 = {2 + 2}")                  # 2 + 2 = 4
write($"uppercase: {uppercase(name)}")      # uppercase: ALICE
write($"list length: {len([1, 2, 3])}")    # list length: 3
write($"null value: {null}")               # null value: null
write($"condition: {10 > 5}")              # condition: true

docs.title: Nested expressions
docs.text:
Expressions can call functions, access attributes, do math — anything that returns a value:

class Point {
    function init(self, x, y) {
        self.x = x
        self.y = y
    }
}

p = Point(3, 4)
write($"Point is at ({p.x}, {p.y})")   # Point is at (3, 4)

docs.title: Escape sequences
docs.text:
Escape sequences in format strings work the same as in regular strings, but only outside { }:

write($"line one\nline two")
write($"tab\there")

docs.title: Difference from regular strings
docs.text: Regular string Format string Prefix none $ Embed expressions no yes, with { } Escape sequences yes yes (outside { })

docs.title: Defining a function
docs.text:
function greet(name) {
    return "Hello, " + name + "!"
}

write(greet("world"))   # Hello, world!
  • return exits the function with a value.
  • A function that reaches the end without a return returns null.

docs.title: Default parameters
docs.text:
Parameters can have a default value. All required parameters must come before defaults.

function greet(name, greeting = "Hello") {
    write($"{greeting}, {name}!")
}

greet("Alice")             # Hello, Alice!
greet("Bob", "Hi")         # Hi, Bob!

function connect(host, port = 8080, secure = false) {
    write($"{host}:{port} secure={secure}")
}

connect("localhost")                # localhost:8080 secure=false
connect("example.com", 443, true)   # example.com:443 secure=true

docs.title: Variadic functions
docs.text:
A ...name parameter collects all extra arguments into a list. It must be the last parameter.

function sum(...nums) {
    total = 0
    for n in nums { total += n }
    return total
}

write(sum(1, 2, 3))         # 6
write(sum(10, 20, 30, 40))  # 100
write(sum())                # 0

Variadic can be combined with regular parameters:

function log(level, ...messages) {
    for msg in messages {
        write($"[{level}] {msg}")
    }
}

log("INFO", "Server started", "Ready")

docs.title: Multiple return values
docs.text:
A function can return multiple values. Use destructuring assignment to unpack them.

function min_max(a, b) {
    if a < b { return a, b }
    else      { return b, a }
}

lo, hi = min_max(8, 3)
write(lo)   # 3
write(hi)   # 8

function coords() {
    return 4, 7, 9
}

x, y, z = coords()

docs.title: Functions as values
docs.text:
Functions are first-class values — they can be stored and passed as arguments:

function double(x) {
    return x * 2
}

f = double
write(f(5))   # 10

docs.title: Closures
docs.text:
Inner functions capture variables from the enclosing scope:

function make_counter() {
    count = 0
    function increment() {
        count = count + 1
        return count
    }
    return increment
}

counter = make_counter()
write(counter())   # 1
write(counter())   # 2
write(counter())   # 3

docs.title: Recursion
docs.text:
function factorial(n) {
    if n <= 1 { return 1 }
    return n * factorial(n - 1)
}

write(factorial(6))   # 720

docs.title: Higher-order functions
docs.text:
function apply_twice(f, x) {
    return f(f(x))
}

write(apply_twice(fn(x) => x * 2, 3))   # 12

docs.title: Lambdas
docs.text: Lambdas are anonymous functions written inline with the fn keyword. They are first-class values and can be stored, passed, and returned like any other value.

docs.title: Short form
docs.text:
Evaluates a single expression and returns it:

double = fn(x) => x * 2
write(double(5))   # 10

add = fn(a, b) => a + b
write(add(3, 4))   # 7

docs.title: Long form
docs.text:
Runs a block with multiple statements. Use return to produce a value:

greet = fn(name) {
    msg = "Hello, " + name + "!"
    return msg
}

write(greet("Luz"))   # Hello, Luz!

docs.title: No parameters
docs.text:
say_hi = fn() => "hi!"
write(say_hi())   # hi!

docs.title: Passing as arguments
docs.text:
Lambdas are most useful when passed directly to a function:

function apply(f, value) {
    return f(value)
}

write(apply(fn(x) => x * x, 6))   # 36
write(apply(fn(x) => x + 10, 5))  # 15

docs.title: Storing in a list
docs.text:
ops = [
    fn(x) => x + 1,
    fn(x) => x * 2,
    fn(x) => x - 3
]

for op in ops {
    write(op(10))
}
# 11
# 20
# 7

docs.title: Closures
docs.text:
Lambdas capture variables from the surrounding scope at the time they are created:

function make_adder(n) {
    return fn(x) => x + n
}

add5 = make_adder(5)
add10 = make_adder(10)

write(add5(3))    # 8
write(add10(3))   # 13

docs.title: Immediately invoked
docs.text:
A lambda can be called right after it is defined:

result = fn(x) => x * x
write(result(7))   # 49

docs.title: Difference between fn and function
docs.text: function fn Name Required None (anonymous) Short form No fn(x) => expr Long form Yes fn(x) { body } Recursion Yes (by name) Not directly Use case Reusable, top-level Inline, callbacks

docs.title: Importing a file
docs.text:
Use import to run another .luz file. Everything defined in that file (variables, functions, classes) becomes available in the global scope:

import "utils.luz"

result = my_function(42)
write(result)

docs.title: Path resolution
docs.text:
The path in import is relative to the file that contains the import statement.

import "helpers/math.luz"
import "lib/strings.luz"

docs.title: Execution model
docs.text:
When a file is imported:

  1. The file is read and executed from top to bottom.
  2. All definitions land in the global scope of the importing file.
  3. The import happens at the point where the import statement appears.

docs.title: Circular imports
docs.text:
Luz automatically tracks which files have been imported. If a file tries to import a file that is already being executed (directly or transitively), the second import is silently skipped.

# a.luz
import "b.luz"

# b.luz
import "a.luz"   # skipped — a.luz is already being executed

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
greetings.luz: 

function hello(name) {
    return $"Hello, {name}!"
}

function goodbye(name) {
    return $"Goodbye, {name}!"
}

main.luz: 

import "greetings.luz"

write(hello("Alice"))    # Hello, Alice!
write(goodbye("Alice"))  # Goodbye, Alice!

docs.title: Defining a class
docs.text:
class Animal {
    function init(self, name) {
        self.name = name
    }

    function speak(self) {
        write($"{self.name} makes a sound")
    }
}
  • init is the constructor. It is called automatically when the class is instantiated.
  • self refers to the current instance and must be the first parameter of every method.
  • Attributes are created by assigning to self.attribute_name.

docs.title: Creating instances
docs.text:
a = Animal("Leo")
a.speak()          # Leo makes a sound
write(a.name)      # Leo

docs.title: Attributes
docs.text:
Attributes can be set on instances at any time:

a.age = 5
write(a.age)   # 5

docs.title: Inheritance
docs.text:
Use extends to inherit from a parent class. The child class automatically inherits all parent methods.

class Dog extends Animal {
    function init(self, name, breed) {
        super.init(name)
        self.breed = breed
    }

    function speak(self) {
        super.speak()
        write("(woof!)")
    }
}

d = Dog("Rex", "Labrador")
d.speak()
# Rex makes a sound
# (woof!)

docs.title: super
docs.text:
super gives access to the parent class's methods from inside a child method. Call parent methods with super.method_name(args).

class Cat extends Animal {
    function speak(self) {
        super.speak()   # calls Animal.speak
        write("(meow!)")
    }
}

docs.title: Method overriding
docs.text:
Defining a method with the same name in a child class replaces the parent's version:

class GuideDog extends Dog {
    function speak(self) {
        write($"{self.name} is a guide dog and stays quiet.")
    }
}

g = GuideDog("Buddy", "Retriever")
g.speak()   # Buddy is a guide dog and stays quiet.

docs.title: instanceof
docs.text:
instanceof(obj, Class) returns true if obj is an instance of Class or any of its subclasses. It walks the full inheritance hierarchy:

write(instanceof(d, Dog))     # true
write(instanceof(d, Animal))  # true  (Dog extends Animal)
write(instanceof(d, Cat))     # false

docs.title: typeof
docs.text:
typeof(value) returns the type name as a string. For class instances it returns the class name:

write(typeof(d))    # Dog
write(typeof(42))   # int

docs.title: Bound methods
docs.text:
When you retrieve a method from an instance, self is automatically bound to it. You can store the method in a variable and call it later without passing the instance:

class Counter {
    function init(self) { self.n = 0 }
    function inc(self) {
        self.n = self.n + 1
        return self.n
    }
}

c = Counter()
inc = c.inc   # bound method — self is already c

write(inc())  # 1
write(inc())  # 2
write(inc())  # 3

This works with any method, including inherited ones. The bound method always refers to the original instance.

docs.title: Polymorphism
docs.text:
Because Luz is dynamically typed, any object with the right methods can be used interchangeably:

class Shape {
    function area(self) { return 0 }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    function init(self, r) { self.r = r }
    function area(self) { return self.r * self.r * 3 }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    function init(self, w, h) { self.w = w   self.h = h }
    function area(self) { return self.w * self.h }
}

shapes = [Circle(5), Rectangle(4, 6)]
for shape in shapes {
    write(shape.area())
}
# 75
# 24

docs.title: Arithmetic
docs.text: Operator Description Example Result + Addition / string concat 3 + 2 5 - Subtraction / unary negation 10 - 4 6 * Multiplication / string repeat "ab" * 3 "ababab" / Division (always float) 7 / 2 3.5 // Integer (floor) division 7 // 2 3 % Modulo 10 % 3 1 ** Exponentiation (right-associative) 2 ** 8 256

docs.title: Compound assignment
docs.text:
Operator Equivalent x += n x = x + n x -= n x = x - n x *= n x = x * n x /= n x = x / n 
total = 0
total += 10
total *= 2
write(total)   # 20

docs.title: Comparison
docs.text: Operator Description == Equal != Not equal < Less than > Greater than <= Less than or equal >= Greater than or equal

docs.title: Logical
docs.text: Operator Description and True if both operands are true or True if at least one operand is true not Negates a boolean

docs.title: Ternary
docs.text: result = "yes" if condition else "no"

docs.title: Operator precedence
docs.text:
From highest to lowest:

**
* / // %
+ -
== != < > <= >=
not
and
or
ternary (if … else)

docs.title: Types
docs.text: Luz is dynamically typed. Every value has a runtime type that is checked when the value is used.

docs.title: Primitive types
docs.text: Type Example Notes int 42, -7, 0 Whole numbers, arbitrary size float 3.14, -0.5, .5 Decimal numbers (IEEE 754) string "hello" Double-quoted, immutable bool true, false Lowercase literals null null Represents the absence of a value

docs.title: Collection types
docs.text: Type Example Notes list [1, "two", 3.0] Ordered, mixed types allowed dict {"key": value} String or number keys

docs.title: Type inspection
docs.text:
Use typeof() to get the type name as a string:

write(typeof(42))        # int
write(typeof(3.14))      # float
write(typeof("hello"))   # string
write(typeof(true))      # bool
write(typeof(null))      # null
write(typeof([1, 2]))    # list
write(typeof({"a": 1}))  # dict

For objects, typeof() returns the class name:

class Dog { }
d = Dog()
write(typeof(d))   # Dog

docs.title: Escape sequences in strings
docs.text:
Sequence Result \n Newline \t Horizontal tab \r Carriage return \\ Literal backslash \" Literal double quote 
write("line one\nline two")
write("column1\tcolumn2")
write("She said \"hello\"")

docs.title: Type casting
docs.text:
Use the built-in cast functions to convert between types:

Function Description to_int(v) Convert to integer (truncates floats) to_float(v) Convert to float to_str(v) Convert to string to_bool(v) Convert to boolean to_num(v) Convert to int or float (auto-detects from string) 
write(to_int(3.9))      # 3
write(to_float("2.5"))  # 2.5
write(to_str(42))       # "42"
write(to_bool(0))       # false
write(to_num("10"))     # 10
write(to_num("3.14"))   # 3.14

If the conversion fails, a CastFault is raised:

attempt {
    x = to_int("abc")
} rescue (e) {
    write(e)   # CastFault: cannot convert "abc" to int
}

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
Variables are assigned with =. No declaration keyword is needed.

x = 10
name = "Luz"
items = [1, 2, 3]
data = {"score": 100}
empty = null

docs.title: Reassignment
docs.text:
Variables can be reassigned to any type at any time:

x = 10
x = "now a string"
x = true

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
Variables live in the scope where they are assigned. Functions create their own scope. Inner scopes can read variables from outer scopes (closures), but assigning in an inner scope creates a new local variable — it does not modify the outer one.

x = 10

function show() {
    write(x)   # reads outer x → 10
}

function shadow() {
    x = 99     # creates a new local x, does not touch outer x
    write(x)   # 99
}

show()
shadow()
write(x)   # still 10

docs.title: Compound assignment
docs.text:
Luz supports +=, -=, *=, and /=:

count += 1
total *= 2
score -= 5
ratio /= 4

docs.title: luz-clock
docs.text:
Date and time utilities included with Luz. No installation needed.

import "clock"

docs.title: now()
docs.text:
Returns a dict with all components of the current local time.

Key Description year Four-digit year month Month (1–12) day Day of month (1–31) hour Hour (0–23) min Minute (0–59) sec Second (0–59) ms Milliseconds (0–999) weekday Day of week (0=Monday … 6=Sunday) yearday Day of year (1–366) 
t = now()
write(t["year"])    # 2026
write(t["hour"])    # 17

docs.title: today()
docs.text:
Returns only the date part: {year, month, day}.

d = today()
write($"{d["year"]}-{d["month"]}-{d["day"]}")   # 2026-3-22

docs.title: current_time()
docs.text:
Returns only the time part: {hour, min, sec, ms}.

t = current_time()
write($"{t["hour"]}:{t["min"]}:{t["sec"]}")   # 17:05:42

docs.title: stamp()
docs.text:
Returns the current Unix timestamp (seconds since 1970-01-01) as a float.

ts = stamp()
write(ts)   # 1774210625.83

docs.title: fmt(pattern)
docs.text:
Formats the current date/time using a pattern string.

Pattern Output %Y Four-digit year %m Month (01–12) %d Day (01–31) %H Hour 24h (00–23) %M Minute (00–59) %S Second (00–59) %A Full weekday name %B Full month name %I Hour 12h (01–12) %p AM/PM 
write(fmt("%Y-%m-%d"))           # 2026-03-22
write(fmt("%H:%M:%S"))           # 17:05:42
write(fmt("%A, %B %d %Y"))       # Sunday, March 22 2026

docs.title: day_name(n) / day_short(n)
docs.text:
Returns the full or abbreviated name of a weekday (0=Monday … 6=Sunday).

write(day_name(0))    # Monday
write(day_short(6))   # Sun

docs.title: month_name(n) / month_short(n)
docs.text:
Returns the full or abbreviated name of a month (1–12).

write(month_name(3))    # March
write(month_short(12))  # Dec

docs.title: is_leap(yr)
docs.text:
Returns true if the given year is a leap year.

write(is_leap(2024))   # true
write(is_leap(2025))   # false

docs.title: since(ts)
docs.text:
Returns the seconds elapsed since a Unix timestamp.

start = stamp()
# ... do something ...
write(since(start))   # 0.003

docs.title: diff(ts1, ts2)
docs.text:
Returns the absolute difference in seconds between two timestamps.

write(diff(1000, 1600))   # 600

docs.title: add_secs(ts, n) / add_mins(ts, n) / add_hours(ts, n) / add_days(ts, n)
docs.text:
Returns a new timestamp offset by the given amount.

ts = stamp()
tomorrow = add_days(ts, 1)
next_hour = add_hours(ts, 1)

docs.title: from_stamp(ts)
docs.text:
Converts a Unix timestamp to a time dict (same format as now()).

t = from_stamp(0)
write(t["year"])   # 1970

docs.title: parse_stamp(date_str, pattern)
docs.text:
Parses a date string into a Unix timestamp.

ts = parse_stamp("2025-01-15", "%Y-%m-%d")
write(from_stamp(ts)["year"])   # 2025

docs.title: to_secs(h, m, s)
docs.text:
Converts hours, minutes, seconds to total seconds.

write(to_secs(1, 30, 0))   # 5400

docs.title: from_secs(total)
docs.text:
Breaks a total seconds value into {hours, mins, secs}.

parts = from_secs(3725)
write(parts["hours"])   # 1
write(parts["mins"])    # 2
write(parts["secs"])    # 5

docs.title: luz-io
docs.text:
File I/O utilities included with Luz. No installation needed.

import "io"

docs.title: copy_file(src, dst)
docs.text:
Copies the contents of src into dst.

copy_file("input.txt", "backup.txt")

docs.title: read_file_or(path, default_value)
docs.text:
Reads a file and returns its content. Returns default_value if the file does not exist.

content = read_file_or("config.txt", "")

docs.title: write_file_new(path, content)
docs.text:
Writes content to a file only if the file does not already exist. Raises an error if the file is already there.

write_file_new("output.txt", "hello")

docs.title: read_lines(path)
docs.text:
Reads a file and returns its lines as a list of strings. Newlines are stripped.

lines = read_lines("data.txt")
for line in lines {
    write(line)
}

docs.title: write_lines(path, lines)
docs.text:
Writes a list of strings to a file, one per line.

write_lines("out.txt", ["line1", "line2", "line3"])

docs.title: append_line(path, line)
docs.text:
Appends a single line to a file.

append_line("log.txt", "new entry")

docs.title: count_lines(path)
docs.text:
Returns the number of lines in a file.

write(count_lines("data.txt"))   # 42

docs.title: read_line(path, n)
docs.text:
Returns the nth line of a file (0-indexed). Raises an error if n is out of range.

first = read_line("data.txt", 0)

docs.title: read_csv(path)
docs.text:
Reads a CSV file and returns a list of rows. Each row is a list of strings. The first row is the header.

rows = read_csv("data.csv")
for row in rows {
    write(row[0])
}

docs.title: read_csv_dict(path)
docs.text:
Reads a CSV file as a list of dictionaries, using the first row as keys.

records = read_csv_dict("people.csv")
for record in records {
    write(record["name"])
}

docs.title: write_csv(path, rows)
docs.text:
Writes a list of rows (list of lists) to a CSV file.

data = [["name", "age"], ["Alice", "30"], ["Bob", "25"]]
write_csv("output.csv", data)

docs.title: luz-math
docs.text:
Mathematical library included with Luz. No installation needed.

import "math"

docs.title: Constants
docs.text:
Name Value PI 3.14159265358979 TAU 6.28318530717959 E 2.71828182845905 
write(PI)    # 3.14159265358979
write(TAU)   # 6.28318530717959
write(E)     # 2.71828182845905

docs.title: factorial(n)
docs.text:
Returns n!. Raises an error if n < 0.

write(factorial(6))   # 720

docs.title: fibonacci(n)
docs.text:
Returns the nth Fibonacci number (0-indexed).

write(fibonacci(10))  # 55

docs.title: gcd(a, b)
docs.text:
Greatest common divisor.

write(gcd(12, 8))   # 4

docs.title: lcm(a, b)
docs.text:
Least common multiple.

write(lcm(4, 6))    # 12

docs.title: is_prime(n)
docs.text:
Returns true if n is prime.

write(is_prime(17))   # true
write(is_prime(18))   # false

docs.title: Trigonometry
docs.text: All functions work in radians. Use to_rad / to_deg to convert.

docs.title: sin(x) / cos(x) / tan(x)
docs.text:
write(sin(to_rad(90)))    # 1.0
write(cos(to_rad(0)))     # 1.0
write(tan(to_rad(45)))    # ~1.0

docs.title: to_rad(degrees) / to_deg(radians)
docs.text:
write(to_rad(180))   # 3.14159...
write(to_deg(PI))    # 180.0

docs.title: log10(x) — base-10 logarithm
docs.text:
write(round(log(E), 4))     # 1.0
write(round(log2(8), 4))    # 3.0
write(round(log10(100), 4)) # 2.0

docs.title: hypot(a, b)
docs.text:
Returns the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs a and b.

write(hypot(3, 4))   # 5.0

docs.title: distance(x1, y1, x2, y2)
docs.text:
Returns the Euclidean distance between two points.

write(distance(0, 0, 3, 4))   # 5.0

docs.title: cbrt(x)
docs.text:
Returns the cube root of x. Works with negative values.

write(cbrt(27))    # 3.0
write(cbrt(-8))    # -2.0

docs.title: lerp(a, b, t)
docs.text:
Linear interpolation between a and b by factor t (0.0–1.0).

write(lerp(0, 100, 0.25))   # 25.0

docs.title: map_range(x, in_min, in_max, out_min, out_max)
docs.text:
Maps a value from one range to another.

write(map_range(5, 0, 10, 0, 100))   # 50.0

docs.title: sum(list)
docs.text: write(sum([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))   # 15

docs.title: mean(list)
docs.text: write(mean([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))  # 3.0

docs.title: median(list)
docs.text: write(median([3, 1, 4, 1, 5]))   # 3

docs.title: variance(list) / std_dev(list)
docs.text:
data = [2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9]
write(mean(data))      # 5.0
write(std_dev(data))   # 2.0

docs.title: luz-random
docs.text:
Random number and data generation library included with Luz. No installation needed.

import "random"

docs.title: random()
docs.text:
Random float between 0.0 and 1.0.

write(random())   # e.g. 0.7341

docs.title: random_float(low, high)
docs.text:
Random float in the range [low, high).

write(random_float(1.0, 5.0))

docs.title: random_int(low, high)
docs.text:
Random integer in the range [low, high] inclusive.

write(random_int(1, 100))   # e.g. 42

docs.title: random_bool(chance)
docs.text:
Returns true with the given probability (0.0 – 1.0).

write(random_bool(0.7))   # true ~70% of the time

docs.title: coin()
docs.text:
Returns true or false with equal probability.

write(coin())

docs.title: random_sign()
docs.text: Returns 1 or -1 at random.

docs.title: random_normal(mean, std)
docs.text:
Gaussian-distributed random value.

write(random_normal(0, 1))   # value near 0

docs.title: seed(value)
docs.text:
Set the random seed for reproducible results.

seed(42)
write(random_int(1, 10))   # always the same

docs.title: pick(list)
docs.text:
Pick one random element from a list.

colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]
write(pick(colors))

docs.title: sample(list, n)
docs.text:
Pick n unique elements from a list (no repetition).

write(sample([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], 3))   # e.g. [4, 1, 3]

docs.title: shuffle(list)
docs.text:
Return a new shuffled copy of the list.

deck = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
write(shuffle(deck))

docs.title: random_list(n, low, high)
docs.text:
Generate a list of n random integers.

write(random_list(5, 1, 10))   # e.g. [3, 7, 1, 9, 4]

docs.title: weighted_pick(list, weights)
docs.text:
Pick a random element using a weight for each item.

items   = ["common", "rare", "epic"]
weights = [70, 25, 5]
write(weighted_pick(items, weights))

docs.title: random_color()
docs.text:
Returns a random hex color string.

write(random_color())   # e.g. "#a3f2c1"

docs.title: random_token(length)
docs.text:
Alphanumeric random string.

write(random_token(16))   # e.g. "gT3kPxZ9mN2wLqYv"

docs.title: random_pin(length)
docs.text:
Numeric PIN string.

write(random_pin(4))   # e.g. "7391"

docs.title: random_uuid()
docs.text:
UUID v4-style identifier.

write(random_uuid())   # e.g. "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-4789-abcd-ef0123456789"

docs.title: roll(sides)
docs.text:
Roll a die with sides faces.

write(roll(6))    # 1–6
write(roll(20))   # 1–20

docs.title: roll_sum(n, sides)
docs.text:
Roll n dice and return the sum.

write(roll_sum(3, 6))   # sum of 3d6

docs.title: roll_all(n, sides)
docs.text:
Roll n dice and return all results as a list.

write(roll_all(4, 6))   # e.g. [3, 6, 1, 4]

docs.title: roll_advantage(sides) / roll_disadvantage(sides)
docs.text:
Roll twice and return the higher / lower result.

write(roll_advantage(20))     # best of 2d20
write(roll_disadvantage(20))  # worst of 2d20

docs.title: chance(probability)
docs.text:
Returns true with the given probability.

if chance(0.1) {
    write("Critical hit!")
}

docs.title: flip()
docs.text:
Returns "heads" or "tails".

write(flip())

docs.title: luz-system
docs.text:
System utilities included with Luz. No installation needed.

import "system"

docs.title: is_windows()
docs.text: Returns true if running on Windows.

docs.title: is_linux()
docs.text: Returns true if running on Linux.

docs.title: is_mac()
docs.text: Returns true if running on macOS.

docs.title: path_sep()
docs.text:
Returns the path separator for the current OS (\ on Windows, / elsewhere).

if is_windows() {
    write("Running on Windows")
}
sep = path_sep()   # "\" or "/"

docs.title: path_join(base, ...parts)
docs.text:
Joins path components using the OS separator.

p = path_join("home", "user", "file.txt")   # home/user/file.txt

docs.title: path_basename(p)
docs.text:
Returns the last component of a path (filename or directory name).

write(path_basename("/home/user/file.txt"))   # file.txt

docs.title: path_dirname(p)
docs.text:
Returns everything except the last component of a path.

write(path_dirname("/home/user/file.txt"))   # /home/user

docs.title: path_ext(p)
docs.text:
Returns the file extension including the dot.

write(path_ext("archive.tar.gz"))   # archive.tar.gz

docs.title: path_exists(p)
docs.text:
Returns true if the path exists (file or directory).

if path_exists("config.txt") {
    write("found")
}

docs.title: run(command)
docs.text:
Runs a shell command and returns its output. Raises an error if the command exits with a non-zero code.

out = run("echo hello")
write(out)   # hello

docs.title: try_run(command)
docs.text:
Runs a command silently. Returns true if it succeeded (exit code 0), false otherwise.

ok = try_run("git status")

docs.title: sysinfo()
docs.text:
Prints OS, hostname, username, PID, and current working directory to stdout.

sysinfo()
# OS:       linux
# Hostname: myhost
# User:     eloi
# PID:      1234
# CWD:      /home/eloi
